title: The case for South Korea to join the G7
gist: With the G7's share of global GDP down to ~43% from 65% in 2000 and the Indo-Pacific rising, the author argues South Korea, a top arms exporter to NATO Europe and semiconductor power, should become the G7's eighth member to keep the forum relevant against Russia and China.

insights.claim: The G7's share of global GDP has fallen to just over 43% from 65% at the turn of the century, putting the forum at risk of fading into irrelevance.
insights.speaker: Edward Howell
insights.so_what: Quantifies the erosion of the G7's economic weight, the structural case for enlargement.
insights.quote: accounting for just over 43 per cent of global Gross Domestic Product in contrast to 65 per cent at the turn of the century
insights.locator: Its global economic share is in decline

insights.claim: An expanded G7 offers a useful alternative to a UN Security Council rendered impotent by Russian and Chinese obstruction of North Korea sanctions and other resolutions.
insights.speaker: Edward Howell
insights.so_what: Positions an enlarged G7 as a workaround for paralysed multilateral institutions.
insights.quote: the latter of which is at its most impotent since its postwar inception
insights.locator: An expanded G7 offers a useful alternative

insights.claim: South Korea is the second-largest arms exporter to European NATO members after the US (tanks, Chunmoo rocket-launchers, howitzers) and aims to be the world's fourth-largest defence exporter by 2030.
insights.speaker: Edward Howell
insights.so_what: Establishes South Korea as a structurally rising defence-industrial supplier to the West.
insights.quote: becoming the second-largest exporter of arms to European NATO member states
insights.locator: South Korea has already strengthened ties with NATO

insights.claim: Korean chaebols Samsung and SK Hynix dominate the global semiconductor market and account for over one fifth of global semiconductor manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Edward Howell
insights.so_what: Underlines Korea's chokepoint role in global chip supply, central to any tech-alliance calculus.
insights.quote: account for over one fifth of global semiconductor manufacturing
insights.locator: Chaebols (conglomerates) such as Samsung and SK Hynix

insights.claim: Seoul pledged in May to launch its first nuclear-powered submarine within a decade as a counterweight to North Korea's nuclear and missile capabilities, and has free-trade agreements with all G7 members.
insights.speaker: Edward Howell
insights.so_what: Signals Korea's deepening military self-reliance and existing economic integration with the bloc.
insights.quote: Seoul pledged to launch its first nuclear-powered submarine within the next decade

insights.claim: Japan's founding-member status is the main hindrance to Korean membership, with election cycles risking revived historical polarisation, though the 2023 Hiroshima G7 invitation showed willingness to move beyond the past.
insights.speaker: Edward Howell
insights.so_what: Identifies the key bilateral blocker to Korean accession and evidence it is softening.
insights.quote: One hindrance to South Korea's potential membership of the Group of Seven has been Japan's presence
insights.locator: One hindrance to South Korea's potential membership

insights.claim: South Korea will host the G20 in 2028 on its 20th anniversary, an opportunity for Seoul to position itself as a global geopolitical and economic actor in its own right, not merely a US supporter.
insights.speaker: Edward Howell
insights.so_what: Flags 2028 as a milestone for Korea's elevated global standing.
insights.quote: In 2028, South Korea will host the G20
insights.locator: Next year, the United States will host the G7
